jokes.text: Can you say 'get bent'?
jokes.setup: Family trying to get baby to say innocent first words
jokes.punchline: Bart suggests teaching baby profanity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character moment - subverting wholesome family activity with casual profanity. Shows his mischievous nature.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: We should teach the baby some nice first words.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, 'mama', 'dada', 'please', 'thank you'...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Or we could teach him some really cool words!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Like what, Bart?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, there's a whole bunch of four-letter words that really pack a punch.

jokes.text: Mr. Rogers says it all the time.
jokes.setup: Bart defending his suggestion of profanity for baby
jokes.punchline: Claims wholesome Mr. Rogers uses profanity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - invoking the most wholesome TV personality to defend profanity. Classic Bart rationalization.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Look, Mr. Rogers uses profanity all the time!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? Mr. Rogers never swore in his life!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, have you ever listened to the album where he goes, 'Won't you be my neighbor, you son of a —'

jokes.text: Shove it. Shove it.
jokes.punchline: Bart persistently teaches more profanity
jokes.explanation: Continuation of the gag, less impactful than the setup but maintains character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Maggie, say 'damn'.

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: *sucks pacifier*

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Come on, it's easy. Damn. Damn, damn, damn.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Now you try.

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: Daddy!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What?!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Aw, man, she called you Daddy.

jokes.text: Did you hear that? She said 'burlap'.
jokes.punchline: Homer interprets it as a specific word
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer - overeager misinterpretation. The specific word 'burlap' adds absurdist touch.

dialogue.speaker: Baby
dialogue.line: Burp

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Did you say Daddy?

jokes.text: 'Tis better to remain silent and be thought a fool than to open your mouth and remove all doubt.' [Thinking] What does that mean?
jokes.setup: Homer quotes wise saying about silence
jokes.punchline: Immediately demonstrates he doesn't understand it
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic reversal - Homer embodies the exact thing the quote warns against. Self-defeating wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, they say silence is golden.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But my gut tells me it's blue cheese.

jokes.text: You better say something, or they'll think you're stupid. Takes one to know one! Swish!
jokes.setup: Bart giving advice to baby about looking stupid
jokes.punchline: Celebrates his own comeback with 'Swish!'
jokes.explanation: Bart's childish pride in his comeback, complete with sports celebration. Character-appropriate humor.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey, don't worry about looking stupid. I do it all the time and it's worked out great for me.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Swish!

jokes.text: ¡Aye carumba!
jokes.punchline: Bart's catchphrase revealed as his first word
jokes.explanation: Perfect character integration - his signature exclamation was his first word, explaining its significance.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Do you remember Bart's first word?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, it was something like 'Dada' or 'Mama'...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts.

jokes.text: Ms. Pac-Man struck a blow for women's rights.
jokes.punchline: Treating video game as feminist milestone
jokes.explanation: Clever cultural commentary - absurdly elevating a female Pac-Man variant to social progress.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Remember when video games were simple? Just a little dot moving around the screen.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That was Ms. Pac-Man, Mom. A female protagonist in 1983!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my stars, you're right! Finally, a woman in the workplace!

jokes.text: A young Joe Piscopo taught us how to laugh.
jokes.punchline: Treating mediocre comedian as comedy genius
jokes.explanation: Cultural reference humor - Joe Piscopo was known as mediocre, so treating him reverently is absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, look! It's that mediocre comedian we treated like a comedy genius in 1983!

jokes.text: Chipwich for sale! Chocolate chip or oatmeal raisin!
jokes.punchline: Absurdly specific ice cream sandwich varieties
jokes.explanation: Minor background humor - the specificity of flavors for street vendor is mildly amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Street vendor
dialogue.line: Get your ice cream sandwiches! We got vanilla between two slices of bread, chocolate between two slices of bread, strawberry between two slices of bread...

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: What about ice cream between two ice cream sandwiches?

dialogue.speaker: Street vendor
dialogue.line: That'll be fifteen dollars.

jokes.text: Youse guys wanna play stickball? Soitenly.
jokes.punchline: Exaggerated New York accent/Three Stooges reference
jokes.explanation: Standard period/regional humor. The Three Stooges reference adds slight elevation.

dialogue.speaker: Street kid 1
dialogue.line: Hey, why I oughta! Nyuk nyuk nyuk!

dialogue.speaker: Street kid 2
dialogue.line: Soitenly!

dialogue.speaker: Street kid 3
dialogue.line: Woop woop woop woop!

jokes.text: That Mike Farrell really boils my 'potata.'
jokes.punchline: Oddly passionate opinion with mispronunciation
jokes.explanation: Character-specific speech pattern with period reference. The mispronunciation adds charm.

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: I cried so hard when Hawkeye left Korea.

dialogue.speaker: Maude Flanders
dialogue.line: Yes, that final episode of M*A*S*H was quite moving.

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: I just think it's so sad that they had to leave all their friends behind.

dialogue.speaker: Maude Flanders
dialogue.line: Indeed. Though I must say, I found the whole series rather un-Christian with all that cavorting.

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, I think M*A*S*H was a beautiful show about the horrors of war.

dialogue.speaker: Maude Flanders
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that's your opinion. Though I personally found it rather depressing. All that talk about 'summa-summa-summatime,' or whatever that theme song was about.

jokes.text: Homer is what grown-ups call me. Call me Daddy.
jokes.setup: Bart calling him by first name
jokes.punchline: Homer's awkward attempt at authority
jokes.explanation: Homer's insecurity about parental authority, trying to sound mature but coming off awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Homer, can I have the car keys?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't call me Homer. When you call me Homer, it means you're in trouble. Call me Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Okay, Dad. Can I have the car keys?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No. And wipe that smirk off your face.

jokes.text: Domer. [Chuckles]
jokes.setup: Homer insisting on being called Daddy
jokes.punchline: Bart deliberately mispronounces Homer's name
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart rebellion - subtly mocking Homer while appearing to comply. The self-satisfied chuckle sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: From now on, I want you to call me Daddy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Sure thing, Dad-dee.

jokes.text: Why, you little-- [Homer strangles Bart]
jokes.setup: Bart's defiance pushes Homer too far
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature strangling response
jokes.explanation: Establishing the iconic father-son dynamic. Physical comedy that defines their relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why you little!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Ah, he'll tire himself out soon.
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual dismissal of chaos
jokes.explanation: Homer's parental incompetence - treating sustained screaming as minor inconvenience.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: AAAAAAHHHHHHHHH!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eh, he'll tire himself out.

jokes.text: They had 30 sons and 30 daughters. What were their names? Dennis, Brianna... Mavis, Brad--
jokes.punchline: Homer forced to improvise 60 names
jokes.explanation: Escalating absurdity as Homer digs himself deeper. The mundane names make it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Come on, Dad! Tell me more details about the story!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh... okay. There was... uh... let's see... there was Jebediah, Obadiah, Ezeekiel, Jedediah, Lzebediah, Bozediah, Cazediah, Davediah, Ezerediah, Fazediah, Gazediah, Hazediah, Izaiah, Jezebiah, Jezebiah... wait, I already said that one... Kezediah, Lozediah, Mezediah, Nezediah, Ozediah, Pazediah, Qazediah, Razediah, Sazediah, Tazediah, Uazediah, Vazediah, Wazediah, Xazediah, Yazediah, Zazediah, Ababiah, Bababiah, Cababiah, Dababiah, Eababiah, Fababiah, Gababiah, Hababiah, Iababiah, Jababiah, Kababiah, Lababiah, Mababiah, Nababiah, Oababiah, Pababiah, Qababiah, Rababiah, Sababiah, Tababiah, Uababiah, Vababiah, Wababiah, Xababiah, Yababiah, and Zababiah!

jokes.text: What were their names? [Groans] Dennis, Brianna... Mavis, Brad--
jokes.setup: Bart demands names for all 60 children
jokes.punchline: Homer struggles with increasingly random names
jokes.explanation: Escalation of Homer's storytelling predicament, relatable parent trap

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, I want you to name all 60 of my children.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: All 60? Okay... First one is Bart Jr.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Second one is... Bart the Third.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Third one is Bartolomew.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fourth one is... uh... Blart.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fifth one is... Bort.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sixth one is... Kwyjibo.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Seventh one is Earmuff.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eighth one is... Zorblax.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not doing this.

jokes.text: The Coast Guard arrested Boy George for scraping the barnacles off his dinghy.
jokes.punchline: Double entendre about Boy George
jokes.explanation: Period-appropriate double entendre. Moderately clever wordplay but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: TV Comedian
dialogue.line: You know, I was thinking about Boy George the other day. They say he's into culture. Yeah, culture... and a few other things! If you know what I mean.

jokes.text: Got your nose. Got your wallet.
jokes.punchline: Escalates to actual theft
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from innocent child's game to actual crime. Perfect economy and character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Got your nose!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey! Give it back!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I got your wallet too.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why you little!

jokes.text: We're gonna start doin' it in the morning?
jokes.setup: Marge says there will be twice as much love
jokes.punchline: Homer's sexual misinterpretation
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer - immediately thinking sexually when hearing about 'more love.' Character-appropriate misunderstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: There will be twice as much love in this house.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! Twice as much sex!

jokes.text: Bye-bye, keys. [Flushing] [Yelling]
jokes.punchline: Bart escalates to flushing Homer's keys
jokes.explanation: Escalation of toilet flushing gag, Bart's destructive creativity continues

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, I flushed your keys down the toilet.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why you little! D'oh!

jokes.text: Who did this? - Baby. Bad baby.
jokes.punchline: Bart blames unborn baby
jokes.explanation: Clever deflection showing Bart's quick thinking and early resentment of future sibling

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart! What happened to the living room?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It wasn't me, man. It was the baby.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What baby? I'm not even pregnant!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Exactly. He's gonna be real mad when he finds out.

jokes.text: The baby can have Bart's crib and Bart can sleep with us until he's 21.
jokes.setup: Need for bigger place with new baby
jokes.punchline: Homer's ridiculous sleeping arrangement solution
jokes.explanation: Homer's logic fails spectacularly - solving space problem by creating worse long-term problem.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We need a bigger place. With the new baby, there's no room.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we can't afford a bigger house.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I got it! I'll sleep standing up in the closet!

jokes.text: My Cousin Frank did it. You don't have a Cousin Frank. He became Francine back in '76. Then he joined that cult. I think his name is Mother Shabubu now.
jokes.setup: Homer defending his sleeping arrangement idea
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd backstory for non-existent cousin
jokes.explanation: Masterful escalation - each revelation gets more absurd. The name 'Mother Shabubu' is brilliantly random.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look, I'm just saying my cousin Ronnie used to sleep like this all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you don't have a cousin Ronnie.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, he died in a logging accident. Very sad. He was working in the woods at night with nothing but a flashlight and a harmonica.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Then a bear came along. Real big bear. Ronnie tried to play it a lullaby on the harmonica, but the bear didn't like country music. So the bear—

jokes.text: Once you get used to the smell of melted hog fat, you'll wonder how you ever did without it.
jokes.setup: Terrible smell in house being shown
jokes.punchline: Agent tries to spin horrible smell as positive
jokes.explanation: Good salesman satire - desperately trying to make negatives sound appealing. Specific 'hog fat' detail helps.

dialogue.speaker: Real Estate Agent
dialogue.line: Now, some people might call this aroma... unpleasant. But I prefer to think of it as... rustic charm. Yes, that's it. Rustic charm. Why, I'd wager this distinctive bouquet adds at least 10% to the property value!

jokes.text: Mmm. Hog fat.
jokes.setup: Agent mentions melted hog fat smell
jokes.punchline: Homer finds it appealing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - finding gross thing delicious. Sets up his signature 'Mmm' pattern.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.line: It smells like melted hog fat.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... melted hog fat.

jokes.text: Why buy a house when ye can buy a houseboat? Arr!
jokes.punchline: Sea Captain's enthusiastic but inappropriate sales pitch
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate enthusiasm for nautical lifestyle. The 'Arr!' sells his personality.

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Ahoy! Welcome to me luxury ocean-front property! She's a real beauty, eh? Built from reclaimed ship materials and the dreams of drowning men. Only thirty thousand dollars, and that includes the free mold inspection—the mold comes with the house!

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Don't mind those wet spots on the carpet. That's just the ocean coming in during high tide. Adds character! And these here portholes? Hurricane-tested. Well, not successfully, but tested nonetheless.

jokes.text: Once we get the cats out, it won't be too bad. Actually, according to the will, the cats own the house. You'd be their tenants.
jokes.punchline: Cats actually own the house legally
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from pest problem to legal absurdity. Unexpected twist on typical 'fixer-upper' issues.

dialogue.speaker: Real estate agent
dialogue.line: I'm afraid the cats actually own the house legally. They've filed all the proper paperwork.

jokes.text: That's real shag carpeting. Arr!
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate carpeting on boat presented as selling point
jokes.explanation: Absurdity of carpet on boat, Sea Captain's enthusiasm for impractical features

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Ar ar ar, me new houseboat's got all the latest features! Shag carpeting throughout the vessel!

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Perfect for when the boat's rockin' and the seas are gettin' rough, if ye catch me drift! Ar ar ar!

jokes.text: I wish we lived in the kitty house. I could've trained them to be my unholy army of the night. Go, my pretties. Kill! Kill!
jokes.punchline: Bart's villainous fantasy about cat army
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart evil mastermind moment. The theatrical 'Go, my pretties!' reference adds flair.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Soon, my feline friends, you will help me take over the world. We shall destroy all dogs and make humans our servants!

jokes.text: Dad, I have a problem. Why'd you come to me?
jokes.punchline: Grampa immediately deflects responsibility
jokes.explanation: Establishes dysfunctional father-son relationship. Grampa's immediate avoidance is character-defining.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad, I need your help with something.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Well, I would, but I'm very tired. Also, I don't know anything about that.

jokes.text: I don't know nothin'. I used to get by on my looks. Now they're gone, withered away like an old piece of fruit.
jokes.punchline: Self-pitying excuse about lost looks
jokes.explanation: Classic elderly vanity joke. The fruit metaphor adds poetic sadness that's both funny and pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I'd give you advice, but I lost my looks and nobody listens to me anymore.

jokes.text: I was voted the handsomest boy in Albany, New York.
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific and probably false boast
jokes.explanation: Specificity makes the boast funnier. Albany, NY as location adds mundane quality to grandiose claim.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I used to be quite the looker. Why, I once turned down a date with Audrey Hepburn.

jokes.text: You didn't build this house. You won it on a crooked '50s game show.
jokes.setup: Grampa claiming he built house with his hands
jokes.punchline: Homer reveals embarrassing truth about house origin
jokes.explanation: Great period detail about corrupt game shows. Deflates Grampa's pride with specific historical reference.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I built this house with these two hands!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Dad, you bought it in 1971.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: And I've been lying about it ever since!

jokes.text: I ratted on everybody and got off scot-free.
jokes.punchline: Proudly admits to betraying others
jokes.explanation: Grampa's moral flexibility revealed. Takes pride in what should be shameful behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't build the house. I lied. But I'm proud of it!

jokes.text: So how long before you shipped Grampa off to the old folks' home? About three weeks.
jokes.setup: Sweet moment of Grampa moving in with family
jokes.punchline: Immediate revelation of his quick abandonment
jokes.explanation: Perfectly deflates the sentimental moment. The casual 'three weeks' makes the betrayal funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Dad, we're so happy you're moving in with us.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Well, I'm ready to be part of this family again.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, Grampa! This is gonna be awesome!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Now, where's my room?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, we set up the basement for you.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: The basement? I'm outta here.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Dad, wait!

jokes.text: I'll tell you about the time I got locked in the bank vault with Mr. Mooney. It was another one of my harebrained schemes. Wait a minute. That was The Lucy Show.
jokes.punchline: Realizes he's describing TV show plot
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about confusing real life with TV. Homer's self-correction shows rare self-awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, I got a great story. So there's this guy, right? And he's married to this beautiful woman, but he's too stupid to realize how lucky he is. So he goes to work at this nuclear power plant, where he sits around eating donuts all day. And his boss is always yelling at him, and his wife is always mad at him, and his kids are driving him crazy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... that's my life!

jokes.text: Hey, Homer, this house sucks. Bart, I told you not to use that word. Call me 'Daddy'.
jokes.punchline: Homer ignores insult, focuses on wrong name
jokes.explanation: Homer's misplaced priorities - more concerned with title than house criticism. Character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: This house is a dump!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's not nice, son. We call it the Simpson estate now.

jokes.text: Buenos días, neighboritos. The handle's Flanders, but my friends call me Ned.
jokes.punchline: Overly friendly greeting with forced Spanish
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned introduction - aggressively friendly with forced hip language. 'Neighboritos' is brilliantly annoying.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, howdy-diddly-do, neighbors! Welcome to the neighborhood! I'm Nedward Flanders, but you can call me Ned. Would you like some lemonade? I made it fresh this morning with purified water and just a touch of sweetener. Nothing too crazy!

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: And I want you to know, we're very diverse here in Springfield. Why, I speak a little Spanish myself. Hola, amigos! Como está? That means 'hello, friends, how are you?' I learned it from a very wholesome educational program.

jokes.text: Who the hell are you?
jokes.punchline: Homer's hostile response to friendliness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - suspicious of friendliness. Establishes the Homer-Ned dynamic immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, howdy-diddly-do, Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, shut up, Flanders.

jokes.text: And that 'little while' is now eight years and counting. [Chuckles]
jokes.setup: Ned saying Homer can borrow TV tray for 'little while'
jokes.punchline: Homer reveals he never returned it
jokes.explanation: Shows Homer's shameless mooching. The casual chuckle shows he's not embarrassed about it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Little while? Ned, I borrowed that TV tray in 1987!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: 1987?! Homer, that was over 30 years ago!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I've been using it every day. It's like part of the family now.

jokes.text: It's not easy to juggle a pregnant wife and a troubled child, but somehow I managed to fit in eight hours of TV a day.
jokes.setup: Homer describing challenges of family life
jokes.punchline: Reveals his actual priority is watching TV
jokes.explanation: Perfect character revelation. Sets up responsible father image then reveals complete irresponsibility.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, being a father and a husband is very challenging. You have to keep the house running, make sure the kids do their homework, help your wife with chores...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But honestly, none of that matters as much as my TV time. That's what really keeps me going.

jokes.text: Krusty Burger is the official meat-flavored sandwich of the 1984 Olympics!
jokes.punchline: 'Meat-flavored' instead of actual meat
jokes.explanation: Brilliant corporate double-speak. 'Meat-flavored' suggests it's not actually meat, just tastes like it.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, I'm proud to announce that Krusty Burger is now the official sponsor of the Olympics!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: And to celebrate, all our burgers will now be made with 'meat-flavored' instead of actual meat.

jokes.text: Coochy coochy coo! - Krusty funny. - Duh.
jokes.punchline: Bart's deadpan 'duh' response to comedy
jokes.explanation: Baby Bart's sophisticated comedy critique shows his character from early age

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey kid, want to see something funny?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Duh.

jokes.text: I almost swallowed some of the juice. I'll be tasting that for weeks.
jokes.punchline: Disgusted by his own product
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity endorsement satire - star disgusted by product they're paid to promote.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Krusty Burger! So good, you'll want to eat it every day! *takes bite* Bleagh! What is this garbage?! *spits out* How do you people eat this stuff?!

jokes.text: Not to worry, Mr. 'K.' We've rigged the cards. They're all in events that Communists never lose. I like. I like.
jokes.punchline: Reveals rigged promotion based on Cold War assumptions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of corporate promotion schemes and Cold War politics

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Listen, this giveaway is costing me a fortune. Nobody's supposed to actually win!

dialogue.speaker: Executive
dialogue.line: Sir, someone did win. They have the winning ticket.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: That's impossible! I rigged it so the Soviet Union would have to collapse before anyone could win!

jokes.text: We've rigged the cards. They're all in events that Communists never lose. Soviet boycott. U.S. unopposed in most events. You personally stand to lose 44 million dollars.
jokes.setup: Olympic promotion planned to avoid payouts
jokes.punchline: Political situation ruins the scam
jokes.explanation: Smart historical joke about 1984 Olympic boycott. Corporate scheme backfiring due to world events.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty's accountant
dialogue.line: Sir, we've set up the Olympic promotion to avoid paying out on those insurance claims.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty the Clown
dialogue.line: Excellent! Those fools will never see it coming.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty's accountant
dialogue.line: There's just one problem, sir. There's been a military coup in the host country.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty the Clown
dialogue.line: So? What does that have to do with anything?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty's accountant
dialogue.line: The new government has cancelled all sporting events and seized all foreign assets.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty the Clown
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Oy!
jokes.punchline: Jewish exclamation of dismay
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective reaction. Krusty's ethnic background adds authenticity to the response.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Oy vey!

jokes.text: Crib! Crib! I'm a baby!
jokes.setup: Being moved to make room for real baby
jokes.punchline: Desperate regression to maintain status
jokes.explanation: Realistic child psychology - clinging to baby status when threatened. Pathetic but believable.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You're getting rid of me? But I'm your son!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, we need to make room for the new baby.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Dad, I can be a baby too!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Waaah! Waaah! I need my diaper changed!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, you're thirteen years old.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Goo goo ga ga! Goo goo ga ga!

jokes.text: All right, I know how to handle this. just use a little reverse psychology. Let's go, Marge. Leave the baby with his little crib.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks he's using clever psychology
jokes.punchline: Obviously transparent attempt at manipulation
jokes.explanation: Homer's misunderstanding of psychology, thinking he's clever while being obvious

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Obviously transparent attempt at manipulation

jokes.text: Ah. Homer J. Simpson, you're a genius.
jokes.punchline: Self-congratulation for basic solution
jokes.explanation: Homer's inflated sense of accomplishment for simple parenting solution

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! I am the greatest problem solver in the world! I solved Bart's bed problem by simply pushing the bed against the wall. Take that, NASA!

jokes.text: I know you like clowns, so I made you this bed.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to solve Bart's crib problem
jokes.punchline: Creates terrifying clown bed
jokes.explanation: Homer's good intentions creating nightmare scenario. Visual must be terrifying based on Bart's reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A clown bed! What could possibly be scary about that?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, no!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, son. I'll make it real cheerful.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ahhhhh!

jokes.text: If you should die before you wake-- [Crazed Laughter]
jokes.setup: Clown bed supposed to be comforting
jokes.punchline: Makes death threats instead
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of children's prayer. Clown bed actively threatening child is genuinely disturbing.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty the Clown
dialogue.line: Hey kid, this bed is supposed to be comforting!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty the Clown
dialogue.line: Sleep tight, or I'll kill ya!

jokes.text: If you should die before you wake-- [Crazed Laughter]
jokes.punchline: Macabre prayer variation with evil laugh
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark subversion of children's prayer into horror movie moment, perfect escalation

jokes.text: Can't sleep. Clown'll eat me.
jokes.punchline: Simple statement of terror
jokes.explanation: Iconic line. Perfect economy and child logic. The simple fear is both funny and relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm sleeping in the car.

jokes.text: women shaving their backs 9,000 miles away... the Americans are heavy favorites.
jokes.punchline: Crude stereotype about East German female athletes
jokes.explanation: Dated stereotype humor that relies on Cold War era prejudices rather than clever observation

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: And here comes the East German swimming team. My God, they're huge! Those women have muscles on their muscles. In fact, I think one of them is a man. No wait, she's just had a lot of steroids.

jokes.text: Many of the other women represent countries that don't have swimming pools.
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of economic inequality
jokes.explanation: Dark economic reality presented matter-of-factly. Highlights absurdity of global competition disparities.

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: And here comes Michael Phelps, neck and neck with Liu Xiang. Liu Xiang has a slight edge, but Phelps is making up ground. Wait, Liu Xiang is pulling ahead again. Liu Xiang wins! What an upset! Phelps is devastated. Well, that's the difference between a gold medal and a lifetime of crippling student debt.

jokes.text: Wow. A baby and a free burger. Could this be the best day of my life? Coming up next, an hour-long episode of Mama's Family. Yes!
jokes.punchline: Homer equally excited about free food and bad TV
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - birth of child ranked equally with junk food and terrible TV. Character-defining moment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Free food and bad TV? Why, it's like Christmas morning!

jokes.text: Enjoy the miracle of creation! - Shut up, Flanders.
jokes.punchline: Homer's rude dismissal of kindness
jokes.explanation: Perfect character contrast - Ned's genuine sentiment vs Homer's crude dismissal

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, howdy-doodly, Homer! I heard the news about the little bundle of joy. Congratulations! May the good Lord bless you and your family with health and happiness during this miraculous time of childbirth!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, thanks Flanders. Now beat it.

jokes.text: I wanna go home. - No, I don't.
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself, remembering home problems
jokes.explanation: Bart's conflicted feelings about creepy clown bed vs wholesome but alien Flanders house

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Man, I miss home. I miss mom's cooking, I miss playing with Santa's Little Helper, I miss my whole family.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Actually, wait. Mom's cooking gave me food poisoning last week, Santa's Little Helper ate my homework, and my family drives me absolutely insane.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, I don't miss home at all.

jokes.text: Iron helps us play!
jokes.punchline: Creepy enthusiasm for nutrition
jokes.explanation: Establishes Flanders family's unsettling wholesomeness. The mechanical delivery makes it creepy.

dialogue.speaker: Todd Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, howdy-diddly-doodly! Time for a nutritious meal! Did you know that this broccoli contains 47 essential vitamins and minerals? I've memorized the nutritional content of every food in this house! *eyes gleam unnaturally* I think about fiber... a lot.

jokes.text: Hope she didn't scare ya. Hello, Joe!
jokes.punchline: She immediately demonstrates senility
jokes.explanation: Standard senility humor. The immediate demonstration after introduction is efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Howdy, folks! I'd like you to meet my grandmother.

dialogue.speaker: Grandma Flanders
dialogue.line: Who are all these people in my house? Get out! Shoo!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Grandma, we're at the church social.

dialogue.speaker: Grandma Flanders
dialogue.line: The church? I thought this was the Piggly Wiggly!

jokes.text: I've already started you... a college fund at Lincoln Savings and Loan.
jokes.punchline: References institution that would later collapse in scandal
jokes.explanation: Dark economic satire - references real S&L crisis that hadn't fully hit when episode aired

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, I've been thinking about your future. We should put your college fund in Enron stock.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's a terrible idea.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What could go wrong?

jokes.text: Yeah, well, Bart can kiss my hairy yellow butt.
jokes.punchline: Homer's inappropriate response to parenting advice
jokes.explanation: Homer's crude dismissal of legitimate parenting concerns. The 'yellow' detail adds cartoon-specific touch.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sibling jealousy? That's nothing. When Bart was born, I was so jealous, I tried to smother him with a pillow.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I'm just being honest like the book says.

jokes.text: Well, Bart, what do you think of little Lisa? I hate you.
jokes.setup: Father presenting new baby to son
jokes.punchline: Immediate, honest rejection
jokes.explanation: Perfect child honesty moment. Direct, unfiltered emotion cuts through sentimental expectations.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bart, meet your new baby brother, Maggie!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ew, no thanks.

jokes.text: 'Where's the beef?' What the hell's that supposed to mean? 'Where's the beef?' No wonder he won Minnesota.
jokes.punchline: Homer confused by famous catchphrase, draws wrong conclusion
jokes.explanation: Homer's political ignorance on display. Doesn't understand catchphrase but assumes it explains electoral success.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: "Dewey Defeats Truman"... Dewey? Truman? I don't get it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, is this about that guy Dewey who beat up Truman? Why would they put that in the newspaper?

jokes.text: The older they get, the cuter they ain't.
jokes.punchline: Cynical observation about aging
jokes.explanation: Classic cynical aunt observation. The grammar ('ain't') adds character voice authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: Patty or Selma
dialogue.line: Well, well, well. Another baby. How wonderful.

dialogue.speaker: Patty or Selma
dialogue.line: You know, the only thing that gets me through the day is knowing that one day, you'll look exactly like us.

jokes.text: You people are pigs! I personally am gonna spit in every 50th burger.
jokes.punchline: Threatens to contaminate food in retaliation
jokes.explanation: Krusty's childish revenge plan. The specific '50th burger' detail makes the threat both credible and random.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: You want to know why I'm losing money on this promotion? I'll tell you why! And if you don't like it, I'm gonna start putting things in the burger that'll make your kids glow in the dark!

jokes.text: I like those odds.
jokes.setup: Krusty threatening to spit in burgers
jokes.punchline: Homer finds 1-in-50 acceptable risk
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - treating food contamination like gambling odds. Perfectly economical punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty the Clown
dialogue.line: You want me to spit in your burger?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: One in fifty? I like those odds!

jokes.text: Please, please, please, please, please! [Howard Cosell] The American, Drederick Tatum... does a triumphant turkey trot over the supine Swede.
jokes.punchline: Overly elaborate boxing commentary with alliteration
jokes.explanation: Parody of pretentious sports commentary style, contrast with Homer's desperation

dialogue.speaker: Howard Cosell
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, what we are witnessing is a spectacular display of pugilistic prowess and positively phenomenal performance!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Howard Cosell
dialogue.line: Homer's hammer-like hands have hardly helped his hapless opponent, whose hopes have been hastily demolished by his heroic adversary.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

jokes.text: Carl Lewis, I could kiss you!
jokes.punchline: Romantic expression of gratitude to athlete
jokes.explanation: Homer's over-the-top gratitude to Olympic athlete for burger winnings, mild character moment

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Thank you for bringing honor to our country. I love you.

jokes.text: She has the reflexes of a young Mary Lou Retton.
jokes.punchline: Comparing baby to Olympic gymnast
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor. Period-appropriate reference but not particularly inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: My goodness! This baby has the grip strength of an Olympic gymnast!

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: I've never seen such coordination and flexibility. Why, she could medal in uneven bars!

jokes.text: Who's cuter now?
jokes.setup: Bart has drawn on baby Lisa
jokes.punchline: Proud of vandalizing sister
jokes.explanation: Classic sibling rivalry escalation. Bart's satisfaction with his mischief is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba! Look what I did to Lisa's face!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's my boy!

jokes.text: Your son shoved this through our doggie door. And since we're returning your second-born... you think you could see your way to returning my TV tray--
jokes.punchline: Tries to get TV tray back while returning child
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to TV tray joke, Ned's passive-aggressive attempt to reclaim property

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Here's your boy, back safe and sound. Well, mostly sound. He may have said some things that would blister the ears of a lesser man.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Thanks, Ned.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I'll just be taking my TV tray back now.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Mr. Flanders, I was using that!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Not anymore, you little scamp!

jokes.text: Bart. You can talk! Say it again, Lise. Bart. Bart, Bart, Bart, Bart, Bart. I'm her first word!
jokes.punchline: She says brother's name, not parents'
jokes.explanation: Perfect emotional payoff. After all the sibling rivalry, Lisa's devotion to Bart is revealed. Genuinely touching and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why did she say Bart first? We're her parents!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Because I'm clearly the favorite.

jokes.text: Say it again, Lise. - Bart. Bart, Bart, Bart, Bart, Bart. - I'm her first word!
jokes.punchline: Bart's triumphant claim to fame
jokes.explanation: Perfect reversal - neglected older brother becomes the favorite, sweet character moment

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm the only kid in Springfield who's been to outer space!

jokes.text: Well, I'm not surprised. Lisa's crazy about you. She thinks you hung the moon.
jokes.setup: Explaining why Lisa said Bart's name first
jokes.punchline: Reveals Lisa's hero worship despite his treatment
jokes.explanation: Emotional revelation that reframes the sibling relationship. Sweet moment that adds depth to characters.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, Lisa said your name first because she looks up to you, Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa looks up to me?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Of course I do, Bart. You're my hero.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But I'm always mean to you.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I know. That's what makes you so cool.

jokes.text: Can you say 'David Hasselhoff'? Dave Hassahoff.
jokes.punchline: Baby mispronounces celebrity name
jokes.explanation: Mild celebrity reference humor. The mispronunciation is cute but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Say 'Oprah', baby. Oprah.

dialogue.speaker: Baby
dialogue.line: O-diddly-dah.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's actually closer to how it's pronounced in Zulu.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Shut up, Lisa.

jokes.text: Can you say 'Daddy'? - Homer. - No, sweetie. 'Daddy'. - Homer. - D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to get Lisa to say 'Daddy'
jokes.punchline: Lisa says 'Homer' like Bart, Homer's frustrated 'D'oh!'
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to running gag, both children refuse to call him Daddy, classic D'oh! ending

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Come on, sweetie. Say 'Daddy.'

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Can you say 'Daddy'? Homer. No, sweetie. 'Daddy'. Homer. D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to get recognition as father
jokes.punchline: Baby only knows him by first name, uses signature exclamation
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to Homer's earlier frustration. The 'D'oh!' is both signature catchphrase and perfect reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Baby
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: You know, Maggie, the sooner kids talk, the sooner they talk back. I hope you never say a word.
jokes.setup: Bart reflecting on baby Maggie's silence
jokes.punchline: Wisdom about talking leading to backtalk
jokes.explanation: Surprisingly wise observation from Bart about childhood development, sweet protective moment

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about Maggie not talking. At first I thought it was a problem, but now I realize... she's got it all figured out. She doesn't talk, so she never has to deal with backtalk. No backtalk means no trouble. No trouble means... peace and quiet. Man, that baby's a genius.

jokes.text: You know, Maggie, the sooner kids talk, the sooner they talk back. I hope you never say a word.
jokes.punchline: Wishes current baby stays silent
jokes.explanation: Classic parental wisdom - recognizing that talking leads to defiance. Homer's lazy parenting philosophy revealed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I wish this baby would stay quiet.

jokes.text: Daddy.
jokes.punchline: She immediately says the word he wanted Lisa to say
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode-ending payoff. The irony is beautiful - the baby he ignores gives him what he wanted most. Only the audience hears it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I wish Maggie would talk.

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: Daddy.
